statement.markdown: Morris Preorder Traversal is a tree traversal algorithm that performs a preorder traversal of a binary tree without using recursion or a stack. Instead, it temporarily changes the tree structure during the traversal. The goal is to implement this algorithm to traverse a binary tree and return the preorder traversal result.
statement.inputFormat:
The first line contains a single integer N, the number of nodes in the tree.
The next N lines contain node values and their children, in the format: 'value left_child right_child' where left_child and right_child are the 0-based indices of the left and right child nodes respectively, or -1 if the child doesn't exist.
The root of the tree is node 0.
statement.outputFormat: A single line containing N space-separated integers representing the preorder traversal of the binary tree.

constraints.description: The number of nodes in the binary tree.
The value of a node in the binary tree.

hints.text: The Morris Preorder Traversal algorithm modifies the tree structure temporarily during traversal. It primarily deals with nodes that have no left child or whose left subtree has been fully traversed.

In a Morris Preorder Traversal, when a node has a left child, you create a threaded link from the node's left child to the node that comes before it in the preorder traversal. This allows you to backtrack without using a stack.

The core technique in Morris Preorder Traversal involves identifying nodes with no left child or whose left subtree has been visited. For nodes with a left child, you find the predecessor node (the rightmost node in the left subtree), and adjust the tree structure to facilitate backtracking.

editorial.title: Morris Preorder Traversal
editorial.intuition: The problem can be solved using a brute force approach by recursively traversing the binary tree in a preorder manner. However, this approach is slow due to the recursive function calls.
editorial.bruteForce: The brute force solution involves recursively visiting the root node, then the left subtree, and finally the right subtree. This approach has a high time complexity due to the recursive calls.
editorial.betterSolution: A more efficient solution can be achieved by using an iterative approach with a stack data structure to store the nodes to be visited.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution utilizes Morris traversal, a technique that temporarily changes the structure of the binary tree to avoid using extra space for recursion or a stack. This approach works by finding the predecessor of the current node and adjusting its right child pointer to point to the current node, allowing for an in-order traversal without extra space.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the Morris traversal algorithm can be proven by showing that it visits each node in the correct order. The algorithm ensures that the left subtree is traversed before the right subtree by temporarily modifying the tree structure to create a threaded binary tree.
editorial.dryRun: A dry run of the algorithm can be performed by manually traversing the binary tree and verifying that the nodes are visited in the correct order.
commonMistakes: Incorrectly updating the predecessor node's right child pointer
Failing to reset the predecessor node's right child pointer after traversal
Not handling the case where a node has no left child
Not handling the case where a node has no right child
Incorrectly implementing the Morris traversal algorithm
